agent_raw: The pilot design cannot support the network-wide expansion recommendation due to three structural confounds. First, pilot merchants self-selected and were predominantly large retailers with dedicated fraud risk teams. This is a fundamentally different population from the broader merchant network. Comparing their fraud outcomes to the rest of the network is comparing unlike groups regardless of calendar-week matching. A pre-pilot difference-in-differences analysis using fraud rates from before the pilot would determine whether the groups had comparable pre-deployment trends. Second, some pilot merchants adopted stronger checkout authentication features during the same six-week period. Better authentication directly reduces fraud by blocking fraudulent transactions before they are submitted for approval — independently of how well the scoring model ranks risk. Without stratifying pilot outcomes by authentication adoption status, the observed fraud improvement conflates model effect with authentication effect. Third, fraud labels are observed only for approved transactions. If model scores led pilot merchants to approve a different fraction of transactions than non-pilot merchants, the resulting fraud rate comparison is not normalized for approval volume. A merchant that approves more transactions will observe different fraud rates than one approving fewer, even at identical underlying transaction risk. These three confounds together make the pilot result uninterpretable as a model effect. A randomized within-merchant test with authentication held constant and consistent approval tracking is needed before network-wide deployment.
all_issues_raised: Merchant self-selection: pilot group systematically different from network
Concurrent authentication improvements confound fraud reduction attribution
Approved-only label observability sensitive to different approval policies across groups
